text: Hello friends. My name is Dr. Vishal Soni and I'll be demonstrating a case of robotic cholecystectomy in a case of acute calculus cholecystitis in a morbidly obese patient.
The video displays the port placement for the robotic cholecystectomy using the DaVinci XI system, with the robotic arms docked from the right side of the patient.
The camera focuses on the Calot's triangle, located approximately five centimeters below the costal margin, as the target anatomy for the procedure.

The optical port is positioned twenty centimeters away, with a linear arrangement of ports. The working ports are separated by eight centimeters from the optical port, and an assistant port is placed eight centimeters laterally.
The surgeon activates the ICG mode, also known as Firefly mode, to visualize the biliary tree, confirming adequate secretion of the dye for enhanced anatomical definition.
The surgeon begins the robotic cholecystectomy by dividing the peritoneum overlying the neck of the gallbladder, similar to the initial steps in a laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
The surgeon divides the anterior and posterior peritoneal layers, followed by sequential division of the fibro-fatty tissue overlying the gallbladder.
The maneuverability of the hook remains a very strong plus point in usage of this instrument over a scissor or a Marylin forceps.

The surgeon encounters the cystic lymph node, known as the lymph node of Lund, during dissection in the lower part of the gallbladder and Calot's triangle, using its presence as an indicator of the nearby cystic artery.
The surgeon performs careful and gentle dissection, meticulously removing small bits of tissue to avoid injury to the cystic duct and cystic artery, which are yet to be successfully identified.

Robotic surgery is a pure visual surgery and the decisions have to be taken by correct identification. ICG that has been used in this case helps in augmenting this particular decision of identifying the anatomy in the perfect most way.
The surgeon uses imaging to delineate the biliary tree, providing a clear view of the anatomical structures. The cystic lymph node is visible, having been pushed aside during the dissection.
The ICG fluorescence highlights the cystic duct, common bile duct, and Hartman's pouch, providing enhanced visualization of these structures.
It is not a very frequent finding, but we encountered it in our case, and it did cause some difficulty, but we could manage it very easily with the robotic platform.
The surgeon further dissects and delineates the cystic duct, carefully manipulating the tissue between the recently hooked cystic artery and the cystic duct.
The surgeon uses the ICG firefly mode to illuminate the surgical field, ensuring that no accessory ducts are present and that the dissected structures are accurately identified.
The surgeon divides the tissues overlying the gallbladder, extending the dissection both anteriorly and posteriorly to free the gallbladder from its peritoneal attachments.
The surgeon divides the posterior peritoneal layer, aiming to extend the dissection as high up as possible.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to further clear tissue near the Calot's triangle region, enhancing visibility and access to the cystic duct and artery.
The surgeon navigates around Hartman's pouch using the robotic platform and ICG imaging to maintain the correct anatomical plane and identify landmarks accurately.
The surgeon confirms the absence of accessory ducts and identifies the cystic artery and cystic duct using ICG mode. The cystic artery is clearly delineated.

The surgeon dissects a long segment of tissue, cauterizing it with a fenestrated bipolar instrument and dividing it with a monopolar hook, ensuring thorough cauterization to prevent post-operative bleeding from the stump.
The surgeon focuses on the cystic duct, freeing it from posterior attachments. A window is created posterior to the cystic duct to facilitate successful clipping.
The surgeon uses Hemolock clips to secure the cystic duct, employing the ICG Firefly mode to clearly delineate the anatomy. The cystic duct is triply clipped and then divided with scissors.

The surgeon has divided both the cystic artery and cystic duct, confirming no accessory or aberrant ducts. The gallbladder is now being freed from its attachments and fibro-fatty tissue at Calot's triangle, followed by lifting it from the liver bed.
The surgeon encounters edema and fluid in the areolar tissue due to inflammation, using these features to identify the correct surgical plane for dissection.

The surgeon navigates potential small blood vessels in the peritoneal fold anteriorly or in the liver bed, using monopolar current for hemostasis, with the option to employ bipolar cautery if necessary.

The surgeon applies dynamic traction to the gallbladder, changing its direction to sequentially open the tissue planes. A monopolar hook is used to divide the fibrous strands connecting the gallbladder to surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a monopolar instrument to separate the gallbladder from the liver bed, maintaining the correct dissection plane to minimize bleeding and avoid encountering blood vessels.

The surgeon carefully completes the dissection and separation of the gallbladder from the liver bed, ensuring no tearing of liver tissue. A final inspection confirms the absence of bleeding or bile leaks, with surgical clips securely in place.
The gallbladder is placed into an endo bag and extracted from the surgical site using standard robotic cholecystectomy techniques.
